title: HTTPS, TLS Identity, Certificate Trust, Proxies, and Downgrade Boundaries · Browser and Node Client Systems · Narrowing Workshop
summary: An accessibility product team integrating a staging browser API must build a first protocol model from narrated fixtures and explain each policy boundary for https, tls identity, certificate trust, proxies, and downgrade boundaries, producing transport trust and certificate evidence record. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch transport trust and certificate evidence record while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress throu…
objectives: Bind HTTPS authority to the requested host, certificate identity, authenticated transport, and application expectation. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Preserve hostname, chain, validity, algorithm, and revocation-policy checks without disabling verification. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Add an intended private trust root or test certificate without replacing or weakening unrelated trust. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK connects to api3.internal.test through proxy.example.test using a private CA and optional client certificate. Verify requested identity, connected endpoint, roots, proxy authority, downgrade policy, and what evidence the browser lab cannot supply.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for bind https authority to the requested host, certificate identity, authenticated transport, and application expectation. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity, keep Https Identity connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.text: accessible staging dashboard SDK connects to api3.internal.test through proxy.example.test using a private CA and optional client certificate. Verify requested identity, connected endpoint, roots, proxy authority, downgrade policy, and what evidence the browser lab cannot supply.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Https Identity condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: OWASP SSRF Prevention and REST Security Cheat Sheets — Controls outbound destination allow-lists, redirect policy, DNS rebinding, network segmentation, response handling, secrets, TLS, validation, logging, and least authority.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Https Identity precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: accessible staging dashboard SDK connects to api3.internal.test through proxy.example.test using a private CA and optional client certificate. Verify requested identity, connected endpoint, roots, proxy authority, downgrade policy, and what evidence the browser lab cannot supply.

For Fetch and TypeScript Commit to the mission model · Workshop Https Identity, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Encryption without authenticated identity is sufficient HTTPS trust. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

options.text: For commit to the mission model, run one convenient Https Identity happy path, omit method, authority, credential, byte, time, redirect, retry, and cleanup boundaries, then accept plausible output. This does not satisfy Https Identity for a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch transport trust and certificate evidence record.
State the Https Identity preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.

Encryption without authenticated identity is sufficient HTTPS trust. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch. That claim fails the commit to the mission model evidence boundary for Https Identity.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Audit the initial evidence packet · Workshop Downgrade Mixed Content
steps.instruction:
workshop case — accessible staging dashboard SDK connects to api4.internal.test through proxy.example.test using a private CA and optional client certificate. Verify requested identity, connected endpoint, roots, proxy authority, downgrade policy, and what evidence the browser lab cannot supply. Competency probe: Reject unintended HTTP downgrades, insecure redirects, mixed content, and credential forwarding across weaker channels. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Downgrade Mixed Content, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Downgrade Mixed Content precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Downgrade Mixed Content, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: accessible staging dashboard SDK connects to api6.internal.test through proxy.example.test using a private CA and optional client certificate. Verify requested identity, connected endpoint, roots, proxy authority, downgrade policy, and what evidence the browser lab cannot supply. Competency probe: Reject unintended HTTP downgrades, insecure redirects, mixed content, and credential forwarding across weaker channels. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Downgrade Mixed Content, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A final HTTPS URL makes an earlier plaintext hop harmless. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Defend the transferred model · Workshop Downgrade Mixed Content

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Reject unintended HTTP downgrades, insecure redirects, mixed content, and credential forwarding across weaker channels. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
